Introduction

Merlin is a free, open-source business management application built on Laravel. It is designed for small businesses that need to receive supplier invoices, issue sales invoices to clients, and maintain a general ledger — all in a single self-hosted system. The core feature is an AI-assisted invoice processing pipeline that reads PDFs, Word documents, spreadsheets, and CSV files, extracts line items using a large language model, and posts them to the correct accounts with minimal manual effort.

What is Merlin

Merlin is a self-hosted, open-source (MIT-licensed) Laravel application for small business financial management. It gives teams a single place to receive and process supplier invoices, send sales invoices to clients, manage a chart of accounts, and run basic financial reports. The central differentiator is the LLM-assisted invoice pipeline: upload a PDF or spreadsheet invoice and Merlin extracts supplier details, line items, amounts, and tax in seconds, then posts to the correct general ledger accounts automatically when confidence is high enough.

Key features

  • Invoice ingestion — upload PDF, DOCX, XLSX, or CSV supplier invoices individually or via a watched folder on disk
  • LLM line-item extraction — Claude AI reads each invoice and returns structured line items, amounts, tax, currency, and a confidence score
  • Confidence-based auto-posting — invoices above the configured confidence threshold post to the ledger automatically; lower-confidence invoices enter a human review queue
  • Suppliers and clients — a shared contact directory used by both the purchasing and billing sides of the application
  • Sales invoices — create and email outbound invoices to clients using customisable templates
  • Recurring invoices — set up schedules that generate and optionally send sales invoices automatically
  • Payment terms — reusable due-date rules that can be attached to suppliers, clients, and individual invoices
  • Chart of accounts — a structured general ledger with account groups and account types; a default chart is seeded on installation
  • Reports — expenses by account, expenses by supplier, and AI extraction performance metrics
  • Roles and permissions — fine-grained access control; every action is gated by a named permission that can be assigned to any role
  • Audit log — all significant actions are recorded with the user who performed them and a timestamp
  • Transactional and campaign email — powered by the NettMail package; sales invoice emails use drag-and-drop Unlayer templates

How invoice automation works

When a supplier invoice is uploaded (or dropped into the watch folder), Merlin runs it through a seven-step processing pipeline orchestrated by the invoice processing service:

  1. Text extraction — the file's text content is extracted; PDFs use a dedicated PDF extractor, while Word documents, spreadsheets, and CSV files are parsed by the paperdoc library
  2. File-type detection — the actual file type is verified using the Magika tool; unsupported formats are rejected immediately rather than passed on for extraction
  3. LLM extraction — the extracted text is sent to Claude with a structured prompt; Claude returns supplier details, line items, amounts, tax, currency, and a confidence score between 0 and 1
  4. Supplier resolution — Merlin tries to match the invoice to an existing supplier by tax number, then by name similarity; if no match is found, a new supplier record is created in a pending state for a staff member to approve later
  5. Account resolution — each invoice line is matched to a GL account using posting history, then an LLM suggestion, with manual allocation as a fallback
  6. Exchange rate fetch — if the invoice is in a foreign currency, the current exchange rate is fetched from ExchangeRate-API and applied
  7. Posting rule evaluation — active posting rules are checked; matching rules can approve and post the invoice automatically without human intervention

See The Invoice Pipeline for full details on each step.

Technology stack

LayerTechnology
FrameworkLaravel 13, PHP 8.4
FrontendLivewire 3, Volt, Flux UI, Alpine.js, Tailwind CSS 3
AuthLaravel Breeze (Livewire stack)
Roles & permissionsspatie/laravel-permission
Audit logspatie/laravel-activitylog
File storagespatie/laravel-medialibrary
Settingsspatie/laravel-settings
Emailnettsite/nettmail-laravel (transactional + campaigns)
Document parsingpaperdoc-dev/paperdoc-lib
File-type detectionMagika (external binary)
AI inferenceAnthropic Claude API
TestingPest + PHPUnit 12
DatabaseMariaDB (MySQL also supported)

Who this documentation is for

This documentation covers two audiences:

  • End users and staff — people who operate Merlin day to day: uploading invoices, managing suppliers and clients, sending sales invoices, running reports. The Expenses, Billing, Accounting, Reports, and Administration sections are written for this audience.
  • Self-hosters and developers — people who install, configure, and maintain a Merlin instance, or who contribute to the codebase. The Getting Started and Core Concepts sections are written for this audience and assume familiarity with PHP, Laravel, and the command line.

Merlin is self-hosted software. There is no managed cloud version: you run it on your own server or local machine and retain full control of your data.
